facts.text: Node.js is single-threaded on the JavaScript execution side but uses libuv to manage a thread pool of 4 threads by default (configurable via UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE up to 1024)
libuv implements the event loop with 6 phases: timers, pending callbacks, idle, poll, check, and close. As of libuv 1.45.0 (Node.js 20), timers run only after the poll phase
Linux epoll, BSD/macOS kqueue, and Windows IOCP are kernel-level I/O multiplexing mechanisms that allow a single thread to monitor thousands of file descriptors for readiness
epoll has two modes: Level-Triggered (continuously generates events while data remains in buffer) and Edge-Triggered (generates event only when data enters buffer)
A context switch costs the equivalent of 5,000 to 10,000 clock cycles on modern processors, or several microseconds
In C++11 and C, a data race (concurrent access to shared memory with at least one write, no synchronization) results in undefined behavior; the compiler can optimize assuming it never happens
The Coffman conditions for deadlock are: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait. All four must be present for deadlock to occur
Compare-and-swap (CAS) is a hardware-supported atomic instruction that atomically compares a memory location to an expected value and swaps it with a new value if equal
The ABA problem in CAS: a thread observes a pointer pointing to object A, but by the time it performs CAS, the data structure is A->B->A again. The invariant is broken but CAS on the pointer succeeds
JavaScript event loop processes microtasks (Promises, queueMicrotask) exhaustively after every macrotask (setTimeout, I/O) before rendering or the next macrotask
Nginx uses a single-threaded event loop per worker process, with CPU affinity (pinned to specific CPUs) to eliminate context switching overhead and scale to 100k+ concurrent connections per worker
Thread-per-request servers incur large memory overhead (each thread stack is 1-8 MB) and suffer from context switching costs; a single thread can only handle one request at a time during I/O wait

An increment operation (++) on a shared variable is not atomic: it compiles to three micro-ops (load, add, store), so two threads can both load the old value, add 1, and write back the same value, losing one increment
Semaphores (counting) control access to a resource pool. A binary semaphore (0 or 1) is equivalent to a mutex; counting semaphores allow N threads to access a resource simultaneously
The C10k problem: handling 10,000 concurrent connections was considered hard until event-driven, non-blocking I/O servers like Nginx and Node.js made it routine on commodity hardware

examples.scenario: Netflix uses event-driven architecture internally to handle millions of concurrent streaming requests without thread-per-request overhead
examples.metric: Reduced memory footprint by avoiding 1 MB+ per-thread stacks for inactive connections

examples.scenario: Nginx at Cloudflare handles ~200 million requests per second across thousands of machines using single-threaded event loops with epoll
examples.metric: 1M+ concurrent connections per machine with < 100 MB memory per 10k connections

examples.scenario: Race condition in a shared counter: two threads increment x (initially 0) to x+1 concurrently. Both load 0, add 1, write 1 back. Final value: 1 instead of 2
examples.metric: Lost 50% of increments due to unsynchronized writes

examples.scenario: Java's java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicInteger uses CAS in a loop: if CAS fails (another thread changed the value), retry. Lock-free, no mutex needed
examples.metric: Avoids mutex contention; outperforms synchronized blocks under high concurrency

examples.scenario: Two threads: Thread A holds Lock 1 and waits for Lock 2. Thread B holds Lock 2 and waits for Lock 1. Neither can proceed—deadlock
examples.metric: System hangs indefinitely; circular wait is present

examples.scenario: Node.js file I/O (fs.readFile) is offloaded to the thread pool; the event loop continues accepting connections. Result: I/O parallelism without multithreading the event loop
examples.metric: Single event loop + 4 thread pool threads can handle 10k+ concurrent file ops

examples.scenario: Linux kernel receives TCP data on a socket. epoll notifies the event loop 'socket 42 has data'. Event loop reads it without polling thousands of sockets
examples.metric: O(1) wakeup per event, not O(n) polling; scales to 100k+ sockets per process

analogies.analogy: Event loop: one cashier at a bank who takes a customer, starts their transaction, then moves to the next customer while the first one's request is processed at the back office (thread pool). Thread pool: one cashier per customer—more cashiers needed, more desks, more memory, more context switching.
Two people updating a shared bank account. Person A reads balance (100), adds 50. Person B reads balance (100), adds 50. Both write back 150. Money lost because both read before either wrote.

A bathroom with one key. Only one person can enter at a time. Others wait outside. Once inside, the person locks the door (acquires mutex), does their business, unlocks (releases mutex), and the next person enters.
A parking lot with 10 spaces. Each car that enters decrements the counter; each car that leaves increments it. When counter = 0, new cars wait. Allows controlled resource sharing.
Two trains on a single track, heading toward each other. Train A stopped at signal waiting for Train B to move. Train B stopped waiting for Train A to move. Neither can proceed.
Instead of checking 10,000 mailboxes every second, subscribe to a notification service that alerts you only when mail arrives. epoll is the notification service.

A vending machine: you insert money and see a price tag (read old value). If the price hasn't changed (compare), your money is deducted and you get the item (swap). If someone changed the price, your transaction fails and you retry.

key_numbers.context: Can be increased via UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE; maximum is 1024
On modern CPUs; several microseconds in wall time
Stack size; limits thread count in thread-per-request servers
For 1M connections, 100 GB–1 TB RAM needed in thread-per-request; much less with event loop
timers, pending callbacks, idle, poll, check, close
Dan Kegel coined the term for handling 10,000 concurrent connections

common_misconceptions.reality: Node.js is single-threaded for JavaScript execution, but libuv manages a thread pool (4 by default) for file I/O, DNS, and other blocking operations. The main event loop runs on one thread.
The thread pool is for blocking operations (fs, getaddrinfo). Network I/O uses epoll/kqueue and doesn't consume thread pool slots. Oversizing wastes memory and increases context switching.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Data races only cause crashes or obvious bugs.
common_misconceptions.reality: Data races cause undefined behavior in C/C++. The compiler is free to optimize assuming races never happen, producing code that works in testing but fails in production under different timing.

common_misconceptions.misconception: If I use a mutex, all race conditions are solved.
common_misconceptions.reality: Mutexes prevent data races on the protected resource, but can introduce deadlocks if not used carefully. Using a mutex doesn't mean the program is thread-safe—logic errors still exist.

A binary semaphore resembles a mutex but isn't identical. Semaphores are for resource counting (e.g., N of M resources available). Mutexes are for mutual exclusion and often support priority inheritance.
For I/O-bound workloads, one thread per CPU with non-blocking I/O outperforms N threads. Context switching, lock contention, and memory overhead hurt thread-per-request models.

common_misconceptions.misconception: CAS is faster than mutex-based locking.
common_misconceptions.reality: CAS is faster under low contention. Under high contention, CAS spins (retries), wasting CPU, while a mutex blocks. The best choice depends on contention patterns.
setTimeout is a macrotask. Microtasks (Promises, queueMicrotask) run before any macrotask. A Promise.then() always runs before setTimeout(..., 0).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Java is immune to data races because of memory safety.
common_misconceptions.reality: Java allows data races. A race won't cause a crash or memory corruption (Java is memory-safe), but you will observe stale or inconsistent values. Java has a well-defined memory model to limit the damage.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All deadlocks can be prevented by avoiding circular waits.
common_misconceptions.reality: Avoiding circular waits prevents deadlock, but it's often impractical. Deadlock avoidance strategies (like lock ordering) are common but must be applied everywhere in the codebase.
